name: Cerrillo Blanco Site - circular via Porcuna
name_original: Yacimiento de Cerrillo Blanco - circular por Porcuna
description: Hike a moderate 4.5-mile (7.2 km) circular route through olive groves and historical sites, including the Mezza Pleca Tomb, near Porcuna.
region_intro: Hiking trails around Campiña Del Norte, located in Jaén, Spain, traverse a landscape characterized by undulating hills, extensive farmlands, and areas rich in olive groves and cereal fields. The region offers varied terrain, including cultivated areas and natural features, providing diverse hiking experiences. It also encompasses historical sites and cultural landmarks, integrating natural exploration with heritage. Best hiking trails around Campiña Del Norte The most popular hiking route is PR-A 510 Cástulo, Desired City Trail, a 9.2 miles (14.9 km) trail that takes 4 hours 5 minutes to complete. This route explores the ancient Ibero-Roman city of Cástulo. Another top favourite among local hikers is Cerrillo Blanco Site - circular via Porcuna, a moderate 4.5 miles (7.2 km) path. This trail includes historical elements such as the Mezza Pleca Tomb near Porcuna. Local hikers also love the City tour circular through the emblems of Linares, a 2.8 miles (4.6 km) trail leading through the urban and cultural highlights of Linares, often completed in about 1 hour 12 minutes. Hiking around Campiña Del Norte is defined by undulating hills, farmlands, and historic towns. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy city walks to moderate trails exploring ancient sites. The routes in Campiña Del Norte are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 90 reviews. More than 300 hikers have used komoot to explore Campiña Del Norte's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Campiña Del Norte?
A: Campiña Del Norte offers a diverse network of over 40 hiking trails. These routes cater to various preferences, ranging from easy strolls through historic towns to more challenging paths exploring ancient sites and natural landscapes.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Campiña Del Norte?
A: The hiking trails in Campiña Del Norte are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.4 stars from over 90 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the region's blend of undulating hills, extensive farmlands, and the opportunity to explore historical sites integrated with natural beauty.

Q: Are there hiking trails for all skill levels in Campiña Del Norte?
A: Yes, Campiña Del Norte provides options for all skill levels. You'll find 18 easy routes, 24 moderate trails, and 1 more difficult option, ensuring there's a suitable path whether you're looking for a leisurely walk or a more strenuous adventure.

Q: What historical sites can I explore on a hike in Campiña Del Norte?
A: The region is rich in history. You can explore the ancient Ibero-Roman city of Cástulo on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983492254/pr-a-510-castulo-desired-city-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;PR-A 510 Cástulo, Desired City Trail&lt;/a&gt;. Another route, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987678571/cerrillo-blanco-site-circular-via-porcuna" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerrillo Blanco Site - circular via Porcuna&lt;/a&gt;, includes historical elements like the Mezza Pleca Tomb near Porcuna. Additionally, many trails pass through or near historic towns and cultural landmarks.

Q: What kind of natural landscapes can I expect to see while hiking in Campiña Del Norte?
A: Campiña Del Norte, particularly along the Camino del Norte, offers a varied landscape. You'll encounter undulating hills, extensive farmlands, and areas rich in olive groves and cereal fields. The region also features well-preserved riparian poplar forests and diverse wildlife, providing a blend of cultivated and natural environments.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Campiña Del Norte?
A: Yes, many of the 18 easy routes are suitable for families. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984646193/city-tour-circular-through-the-emblems-of-linares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;City tour circular through the emblems of Linares&lt;/a&gt; is a shorter, easy walk that allows families to explore urban and cultural highlights without significant difficulty.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Campiña Del Norte?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on many outdoor trails in Campiña Del Norte, especially those traversing natural landscapes and farmlands. However, it's always advisable to keep them on a leash, particularly in areas with livestock or near historical sites, and to check specific trail regulations if available.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Campiña Del Norte?
A: Yes, several circular routes are available, offering convenient starting and ending points. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987678571/cerrillo-blanco-site-circular-via-porcuna" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerrillo Blanco Site - circular via Porcuna&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984646193/city-tour-circular-through-the-emblems-of-linares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;City tour circular through the emblems of Linares&lt;/a&gt;, which are popular choices for exploring the region's history and culture.

Q: When is the best time of year to go hiking in Campiña Del Norte?
A: The spring months are particularly beautiful for hiking in Campiña Del Norte, as the olive groves, sunflower, and cereal fields are vibrant with color. Autumn also offers pleasant temperatures and stunning scenery. While summer can be warm, coastal areas provide a refreshing breeze. Winter hiking is possible, but some higher or more exposed routes might require appropriate gear.

Q: What cultural landmarks can I visit near the hiking trails?
A: Beyond the trails, you can visit significant cultural landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5302945" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of the Virgin of Linarejos&lt;/a&gt;, which is featured in several loop trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14622268" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument to Andrés Segovia – Sanctuary of the Virgin of Linarejos loop from Linares&lt;/a&gt;. The region also encompasses historic towns like Linares, offering a rich cultural experience.

Q: Are there any natural attractions or unique phenomena to see?
A: While Campiña Del Norte is known for its rolling hills and agricultural landscapes, the broader region, particularly along the Camino del Norte, features dramatic coastlines and unique natural phenomena like the Bufones de Arenillas, which are blowholes that shoot seawater high into the air. Hikers can also find well-preserved riparian forests with diverse wildlife.

Q: Are there shorter, easier walks for a quick outing?
A: Absolutely. For a quick and easy outing, consider routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984646193/city-tour-circular-through-the-emblems-of-linares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;City tour circular through the emblems of Linares&lt;/a&gt;, which is 2.8 miles (4.6 km) and takes about 1 hour 12 minutes. Another easy option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38140487" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of the Virgin of Linarejos – Doña Luci Gardens loop from Estadio municipal de Linarejos&lt;/a&gt;, a 4.7-mile (7.5 km) path.

Q: What are some longer hiking options for experienced hikers?
A: For those seeking a longer challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983492254/pr-a-510-castulo-desired-city-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;PR-A 510 Cástulo, Desired City Trail&lt;/a&gt; covers 9.2 miles (14.9 km) and typically takes over 4 hours. Another substantial option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14622314" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of the Virgin of Linarejos – Chimney of the Cruz loop from Antigua Estación de Madrid&lt;/a&gt;, which is 10.8 miles (17.4 km) long.
tips: It was built between 1872 and 1910, after the collapse of the Calatrava Gothic church of Santa María la Mayor, of which the sacristy remained, which had been reformed in the 17th century by Benito del Castillo.

Extraordinary piece to understand not only the importance of the "oppidum" túrdulo de Porcuna, but also the history of Alta Andalucía itself. The set of sculptures located in this settlement is, by far, the most important and monumental that Turdetan and Túrdula archeology has provided, and the key to understanding this culture at the time of its full consolidation, during the 5th century BC.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Cerrillo Blanco Site?
faqs.answer: While there isn't a dedicated trailhead parking lot for a formal hiking trail, you can typically find parking within the town of Porcuna. From there, the archaeological site is approximately 3 kilometers away, accessible via rural paths or roads. If driving directly to the site, look for available space near the Interpretation Center, though specific parking facilities are not detailed.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for dogs?
faqs.answer: The route itself, traversing rural paths and olive groves, is generally dog-friendly. However, if you plan to visit the Cerrillo Blanco Archaeological Site itself, it's advisable to check specific regulations regarding pets within the archaeological park area, as rules can vary for protected cultural sites.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this circular route?
faqs.answer: The best time to explore this area is typically during the spring (March to May) or autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder, making for a more comfortable walk through the agricultural landscape. Summers can be very hot in Jaén province, and winters can bring rain, potentially making some paths muddy.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee to visit the Cerrillo Blanco Archaeological Park?
faqs.answer: Accessing the general area around the Cerrillo Blanco Site for a walk does not typically require permits or fees. However, if you wish to enter the specific archaeological park or any associated interpretation centers, there might be an entrance fee or specific visiting hours. It's recommended to check with local tourism information for the most current details regarding the Cerrillo Blanco Archaeological Site.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily takes you through an agricultural landscape dominated by olive groves, characteristic of the Jaén province. You'll likely walk on rural paths and roads. While much of the walk from Porcuna to the site is relatively easy, be aware that the path from the Interpretation Center to the archaeological site itself (about 350 meters) is described as quite steep and can become muddy after rain.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: This route offers a blend of cultural history and rural scenery. In Porcuna, you can visit the impressive Church of the Assumption of Our Lady. The primary highlight is the Mezza Pleca Tomb at the Cerrillo Blanco Archaeological Site, known for its significant Iberian sculptures. The journey between these points provides views of the extensive olive groves.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: The overall walk from Porcuna to the Cerrillo Blanco Site is relatively easy and can be suitable for families. However, the 350-meter path from the Interpretation Center to the archaeological site is steep, which might be challenging for very young children or those with strollers. Consider this section's difficulty when planning with family.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this circular route incorporates sections of several local paths. You will find yourself on parts of the 'Cerrillo Blanco' path, as well as 'Camino a Alharilla' and the 'VP Cordel de Marmolejo'. These are local rural tracks that connect different parts of the agricultural landscape around Porcuna.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like restrooms or water sources along the route?
faqs.answer: As this is not a formally established hiking trail, there are no dedicated restrooms or water sources directly along the rural paths. It's best to use facilities and stock up on water in Porcuna before you start your walk. There might be facilities available at the Cerrillo Blanco Interpretation Center if it is open during your visit.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the rural and potentially steep sections, comfortable walking shoes are essential. Bring sufficient water, especially during warmer months, as there are no water sources on the route. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is advisable due to the open agricultural landscape. If visiting the archaeological site, consider bringing a camera to capture the historical significance.

faqs.question: What is the historical significance of the Cerrillo Blanco Site?
faqs.answer: The Cerrillo Blanco Site is a highly significant archaeological area, primarily known as a necropolis dating back to the 7th century BC. It is the discovery site of crucial Iberian sculptures from the 5th century BC, which are considered among the most important findings in Iberian archaeology. These artifacts offer deep insights into the Iberian culture and the history of Alta Andalucía.
